items.title: Fine Arts Building Welcomes Groundswell Coffee In Space Next To Former Artist’s Cafe
items.summary: DOWNTOWN - Chicago-based Groundswell Coffee Roasters is opening inside the Fine Arts Building this month, seven years after the building's original cafe closed. Located at 410 S.

items.title: Want To Watch The World Cup At Recess? Get In Line
items.summary: Fans from all over the world have been flocking to the West Loop bar, with its massive patio and jumbotron screens, despite the lines that stretch around the block. The bar's guest count is up 300 percent.

items.title: Bud Rita's Opens Loop's First Weed Shop As City Eases Restrictions For Downtown Dispensaries
items.summary: LOOP - The Loop’s first legal cannabis dispensary is now open, two years after Downtown Ald. Bill Conway (34th) changed city rules to make it easier for weed shops to fill vacant storefronts in the Central Business District. Bud & Rita’s, located inside a former Bank of America at 105 W.

items.title: Meet The Man Behind The Sky Ride Tap, The Loop's Last Real Dive Bar
items.summary: THE LOOP - If you've ever been to the Sky Ride Tap, you've undoubtedly seen Kyriakos "Carl" Damianides. Since he bought the bar in 1972, he's been a near-constant presence inside. "For the first 25 years, I worked from 7 a.m. to 2 a.m., seven days a week," he said.

items.title: Chicago Vegan Farmers Market Moving To West Loop
items.summary: WEST LOOP - Chicago's only all-vegan farmers market is moving to the West Loop, with weekly markets starting Friday in Skinner Park. The Chicago Vegan Farmers Market launched last year in West Town and is relocating to Skinner Park, 1331 W. Monroe St., organizers announced in a news release.

items.title: Shedd Aquarium Rescuing 10 Beluga Whales From Shuttered Canadian Park
items.summary: The beluga whales will be flown to their new homes as part of an international rescue effort unprecedented in scale, according to Dr. Karisa Tang, vice president of animal health at the Shedd.

items.title: 30 Things To Do In Chicago This Weekend: Taste Of Chicago, Square Roots, Chosen Few DJs And More
items.summary: Windy City Smokeout, MUBI Fest, the Beverly-Morgan Park Garden Walk and multiple markets are also on deck.

items.title: Chief Keef Will Perform In Chicago For 1st Time In 14 Years With Show At Northerly Island
items.summary: After effectively being blacklisted from performing in the city for over a decade, the South Side rapper will return to Chicago on Sep. 12 as part of his nationwide tour for his latest mixtape, "Skeletor."

items.title: 'Live Launch' brings a modern mall to the West Loop
items.summary: A new retail concept called Live Launch has opened at 810 W. Washington Blvd, blending online and in-person shopping. Eleven storefronts of online-based brands share a warehouse-like space where you can touch, try on, and check products, then scan a QR code to buy. Current tenants include the Chicago Sky's first standalone shop, Lux & Nyx handbags, and Surreal Cycling.

items.title: City Council panel rejects millionaire-tax ballot question
items.summary: The City Council Rules Committee advanced seven non-binding referendum questions for the November and February ballots but turned down Mayor Brandon Johnson's bid to ask voters about a 3 percent tax on millionaires, per the Tribune. The advisory questions let voters weigh in without changing law directly.

items.title: Illinois officials watch mail-ballot rules ahead of 2026 vote
items.summary: Illinois Democrats and Attorney General Kwame Raoul are pressing the U.S. Postal Service over proposed changes to mail-in voting, after a federal judge blocked parts of a Trump executive order on elections. Mail ballots made up roughly 30 percent of votes cast in 2024, so any change to processing timelines could affect voters before the 2026 midterms.

items.title: Mayor names new city public health commissioner
items.summary: Mayor Brandon Johnson has tapped Dr. Garth Walker, chief medical officer at Rush Health, to lead the Chicago Department of Public Health. Walker would replace Dr. Olusimbo Ige, who resigned in May. The appointment requires City Council approval.

items.title: Ground broken on Chicago Torture Justice Memorial
items.summary: Survivors, advocates, Mayor Johnson and Ald. Jeanette Taylor broke ground in Washington Park on the future Chicago Torture Justice Memorial, honoring victims of police torture under Commander Jon Burge and his 'Midnight Crew' from 1972 to 1991. The 2015 city reparations package that recognized the torture included a formal apology, a $5.5 million victims fund, and a CPS curriculum.

items.title: Illinois health officials track a stomach-bug outbreak
items.summary: State health officials are investigating an outbreak of cyclosporiasis, a gastrointestinal illness caused by a microscopic parasite spread through contaminated food. As of Tuesday, 141 cases had been reported. Past outbreaks have been linked to raspberries, basil, salad mixes and cilantro, so experts recommend washing produce under running water before eating.

items.title: CTA reports crime down six months in a row
items.summary: Crime on the CTA has dropped for six consecutive months amid a security surge, the agency's leadership says, per the Sun-Times. The trend covers the transit lines and stations that serve the West Loop, Loop and South Loop.

items.title: Dr. Lisa Nee makes her run for mayor official
items.summary: Cardiologist and VA whistleblower Dr. Lisa Nee formally announced her 2027 mayoral campaign. While working at Hines VA Hospital she spoke out about unnecessary procedures, delays in care and mismanagement, and says she faced retaliation for doing so. She joins a crowded field that already includes Alexi Giannoulias, Matthew Brewer, Susana Mendoza and Mike Quigley.

items.title: Chicago pays double the suburbs to remove lead water lines
items.summary: An Axios analysis finds Chicago pays about $31,000 to replace a single lead service line while Evanston spends under $15,000, Elgin $10,950 and a Riverside bid came in at $9,200, all using the same local contractors. Chicagoans will shoulder much of the estimated $12 billion cost of removing a record 400,000 lead lines over the next two decades. City water officials did not respond to requests for an explanation of the gap.

items.title: City budget fight sharpens over $130 million gap claim
items.summary: Mayor Brandon Johnson says the alternative budget provisions pushed by a group of aldermen he calls the 'corporate caucus' come up $130 million short. Ald. Matt O'Shea countered that the administration is sabotaging the alternative plan. The dispute sets the tone for a contentious fall budget season at City Hall.

items.title: Police department blows past budget for fifth straight year
items.summary: The Chicago Police Department overspent its 2025 budget by $162.5 million, the fifth consecutive year of overruns, with more than $120 million of the overage going to personnel costs, per WTTW. The Obama Presidential Center also releases a new batch of tickets today.

items.title: Navy Pier offers free Centennial Wheel rides after foggy Fourth
items.summary: Navy Pier is giving away free Centennial Wheel rides to make up for the July 4th fireworks show that fog largely obscured. Free-ride tickets are available through Navy Pier while the offer lasts.

items.title: Storm cleanup: what Chicago homeowners and drivers need to know
items.summary: Heavy July Fourth weekend storms knocked out power for nearly 300,000 ComEd customers, flooded homes and CTA buses, and downed trees across the city, and Chicago's Deep Tunnel floodwater system came close to full for the first time in its history. Axios rounds up the insurance basics: if a tree hits your house or car, contact your insurer promptly and document the damage with photos, and remember property insurance typically covers removal and repair only when a structure is actually hit.

items.title: Giannoulias urges residents to report license-plate tampering by federal agents
items.summary: Illinois Secretary of State Alexi Giannoulias is asking residents to report immigration agents who remove or obscure vehicle license plates, after a video appearing to show an agent with a plateless SUV went viral. He said operating a vehicle without the required plates is illegal in Illinois for everyone, and pointed residents to a tip line at 312-814-1730 and platewatch@ilsos.gov.

items.title: Illinois becomes the first state to require independent AI safety audits
items.summary: Gov. JB Pritzker signed the Artificial Intelligence Safety Measures Act, which supporters call the nation's strongest state-level AI safety law. It requires the largest AI developers (frontier labs with more than $500 million in revenue) to publish risk frameworks, undergo annual third-party audits, and report serious safety incidents within 72 hours. Attorney General Kwame Raoul's office will enforce it, with penalties up to $1 million for a first violation. The bill passed the House 110-0 and the Senate 52-5.

items.source_name: Indivisible Greater West Loop (Weekly Action Plan)
items.title: 'Good Trouble Lives On' weekend of action, July 17-19
items.summary: Indivisible Greater West Loop is joining a national weekend honoring the late Rep. John Lewis and voting rights, July 17-19. Local events include a candlelight vigil near Buckingham Fountain (Fri, July 17), plus a voter registration table at Mary Bartelme Park and a volunteer shift at State Sen. Lakesia Collins' Summer Block Party in North Lawndale (Sat, July 18).

items.source_name: Indivisible Greater West Loop (Weekly Action Plan)
items.title: IGWL mid-July calendar: book club, food depository, ice cream social and more
items.summary: Upcoming with Indivisible Greater West Loop: Book Club (July 8), volunteering at the Greater Chicago Food Depository (Fri, July 10, 12:45-3:30pm), a family-friendly Popsicle & Ice Cream Social (Sat, July 11, 11am-1pm), the July Chapter Meetup with guest speaker Nick Uniejewski (Mon, July 13, 6-8pm), and Drinks for Democracy postcard writing (Wed, July 15). IGWL also tables at the West Loop Farmers Market on Saturdays, 8-11:30am.

items.title: Taste of Chicago returns to Grant Park, July 8-12
items.summary: Taste of Chicago is back in Grant Park (Columbus Drive from Balbo to Monroe) July 8-12, with dozens of food vendors, nationally recognized musical performances, local artists, family attractions, and culinary demonstrations across the festival grounds.

items.title: 'Youth Voice, Real Change' Summit at UIC, July 25
items.summary: Registration is open through July 17 for the Chicago Youth Summit on Saturday, July 25 at 11am at UIC Student Center East (750 S. Halsted). The afternoon focuses on youth leadership, creativity, and community impact, with space for young people to connect with peers and explore ways to lead change in their communities.

items.title: Police Superintendent Larry Snelling announces retirement
items.summary: CPD Superintendent Larry Snelling will retire July 15 after more than three decades with the department. Mayor Johnson's first pick to lead CPD, Snelling oversaw a historic drop in homicides and closer collaboration with violence-prevention groups. Fred Waller will serve as interim superintendent, reprising the role he held after David Brown's 2023 exit.

items.title: The July 1 Skyline is out: heat wave edition heads into the holiday weekend
items.summary: Thomas O'Gorman's latest Skyline arrived ahead of Independence Day, with the editor riding out the tropical heat wave 'in front of the air conditioner, set on 67 degrees.' The weekly covers the Loop, River North, Streeterville, Gold Coast, and Old Town; the full paper is available from Inside Publications.

items.source_name: The WCA Weekly (West Central Association Chamber of Commerce)
items.title: Guinness Open Gate Brewery Chicago joins the neighborhood at 901 W. Kinzie
items.summary: The official home of Guinness in Chicago - a locally inspired brewery, taproom, restaurant, and gathering space - has joined the West Central Association. It features Chicago-brewed beers, special releases, weekend brunch, and event space. WCA members get 10% off restaurant visits.

items.source_name: The WCA Weekly (West Central Association Chamber of Commerce)
items.title: Business openings roundup: H&H Bagels, Labriola late-night window, new showroom & more
items.summary: New York's H&H Bagels is now open in Fulton Market. Labriola opened a late-night window in Fulton Market this week. Mannington Commercial opened a new Fulton Market showroom. Stepping Stones West Loop, a Reggio-inspired preschool for ages 6 weeks to 5 years, is now open. The GYN & Sexual Medicine Collective, a women's health practice led by Dr. Sameena Rahman, opened at 312 N. May St.

items.source_name: The WCA Weekly (West Central Association Chamber of Commerce)
items.title: July at the Epiphany Center: free Blues Friday, gallery closings, jazz & more
items.summary: Epiphany Center's July lineup: free Blues Friday with Ms. Sassy plus closing receptions across all six art galleries (Jul 3), the Dan Merlo Quartet's 'Dan Zeppelin' Led Zeppelin reimagining (Jul 9), vocal jazz duo Anne and Mark Burnell (Jul 16), and GRAMMY-nominated trumpeter Keyon Harrold (Jul 25). The Charity Divvy Relay during the xXx Racing Fulton Market Grand Prix is also open for team sign-ups, supporting bike safety and access programs.

items.source_name: The WCA Weekly (West Central Association Chamber of Commerce)
items.title: Construction begins on 33-story residential tower at 410 N. Elizabeth
items.summary: Tree Street Group and Magellan Development have started construction on 410 N. Elizabeth, a 383-unit, 33-story multifamily high-rise in Fulton Market, with Clark Construction as general contractor. Street closure details will be announced - watch for traffic impacts in the area.

items.title: The 78 development: $425M taxpayer subsidy request under scrutiny
items.summary: The current TIF request for The 78 / Chicago Fire stadium is $425 million, focused on streets and a parking lot. The plan would draw nearly $280 million from the Canal Congress TIF - the fund that supports Union Station, Ogilvie, and the soon-to-be publicly owned Greyhound station - raising questions for residents about whether public transportation and other community benefits stay prioritized.

items.title: CAPS public-safety meetings this week (1st District)
items.summary: Three CAPS meetings are scheduled: Beat 113 on July 1 at 5pm and Beat 111 on July 1 at 6pm (both at Capital One Cafe, 100 S. State), and Beat 112 on July 2 at 12pm (walking meeting - email CAPS.001District@chicagopolice.org for location). CAPS meetings connect neighbors, police, and the ward team on local safety.

items.title: Farmers markets across the ward: West Loop, South Loop, Daley Plaza & The Green
items.summary: Green City Market West Loop runs Saturdays 8am-1pm at 900 W. Monroe (ward team on site 9-11am, giving away ComEd storm-preparedness kits this week). South Loop Farmers Market: Saturdays 9am-1pm at Printers Row Park. Daley Plaza Market: Thursdays 7am-2pm. The Farmer at the Green: Tuesdays 11am-3pm at 320 S. Canal.

items.title: Infrastructure roundup: resurfacing done on Carpenter & Polk; bump-outs and lighting in progress
items.summary: Street resurfacing completed on the 900 block of S. Carpenter and W. Polk (Wells to Financial). Bump-out installations in progress at Polk & LaSalle and Polk & Financial, with Jefferson & Tilden starting ~6/26 and Adams & Peoria ~7/13. New residential lighting underway on W. Flournoy and W. Polk. IDOT weekend closures of the Ohio/Ontario feeder ramps continue (June 26, July 17).

items.title: West Central Association small business survey (with UIC)
items.summary: The West Central Association is partnering with UIC researchers to understand challenges facing small businesses on the Near West Side - workforce, costs, customer trends, public safety, and growth. The survey takes a few minutes and will inform future programs and resources.

items.title: Volunteer with Hope's Table at the Chicago Temple
items.summary: Hope's Table, sponsored by First United Methodist Church at the Chicago Temple, serves Chicagoans who are homeless or hungry. Saturday morning services include hot meals, food to go, clothing distribution, and legal support through DePaul's Neighborhood Legal Assistance Program, with Grace Episcopal Church providing space at 637 S. Dearborn.

items.title: City Council approves acquisition of Greyhound station at 630 W. Harrison
items.summary: City Council approved the City's acquisition of the Greyhound station, which serves nearly half a million travelers a year. The move preserves affordable intercity bus service, and City departments are expected to address the station's persistent public-safety problems before the City takes ownership in August - an issue neighbors have raised for years.

items.title: Raoul praises Supreme Court ruling upholding birthright citizenship
items.summary: Illinois AG Kwame Raoul, part of the multistate suit against the executive order denying citizenship to many born to non-citizens, called the ruling legally significant and personally resonant - he was born in Chicago to a Haitian mother not yet naturalized. Rep. Delia Ramirez praised the decision; Gov. Pritzker and Sen. Durbin warned of continued challenges to immigration policy.

items.title: Supreme Court takes up challenge to Cook County's assault weapons ordinance
items.summary: The U.S. Supreme Court agreed to hear a challenge to Cook County's 1996 ordinance banning the sale, possession, and transfer of assault weapons and large-capacity magazines. State's Attorney Eileen O'Neill Burke said the county 'will not back down' from defending the law. Briefing is expected during the 2026 term.

items.title: Chicago and Cook County minimum wage increases take effect July 1
items.summary: The annual Chicago and Cook County minimum wage hikes took effect today, July 1. Also in the roundup: Pritzker issued a disaster proclamation for 11 storm-hit counties including Cook, and a bill on the governor's desk would raise state payments to the wrongfully convicted to $50,000 per year incarcerated.
